title: Joseph of Arimathie
summary: The repository artifact is the pinned CME XML for University of Michigan item AHA2700.0001.001. Its verbal source is Walter W. Skeat's 1871 edition: the title page and the 709-line poem on printed pp. 1–23, while the XML retains 148 page-break links and placeholders for omitted matter across the full volume. Skeat states that George Parker first prepared a transcript from the sole Vernon Manuscript witness and that Skeat carefully revised that working transcript before printing it, so the graph preserves Parker's transcript between edition and witness. The witness is acephalous because f. 402 is lost, but Skeat judged its ending intact; its exact current locus is ff. 403ra–404vb. A complete 710-canvas Digital Bodleian surrogate is cached, with Joseph mapped to canvases 684–687 and only the Joseph portion of the shared f. 404v end canvas included in the work boundary before the following Life of Pilate and Judas. The medieval work, physical witness, working transcript, edition, scan, OCR derivative, U-M encoding, and checked-in artifact remain distinct, as do their rights layers.
review_notes: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English poem, not Joseph of Arimathea as a person, the whole Vernon Manuscript, Skeat's whole four-text volume, its appendices, or any digital carrier.

The 709 count records extant Skeat/U-M line positions. It is not a physical manuscript-line count or a reconstruction of the missing opening; Skeat's fewer-than-one-hundred missing lines and approximately 800-line original are historical estimates only.
The witness boundary is asymmetric: the opening is defective after loss of f. 402, while the surviving end appears complete.
Parker's working transcript is modeled as an intermediary because Skeat expressly says Parker made the transcript and Skeat then carefully revised the printed copy.

The complete Bodleian bundle covers all 710 provider canvases. The target occupies only canvases 684–687, and the final canvas is shared: Joseph ends in f. 404vb before the next Pilate/Judas item begins in the same column.
The 34 XML LG elements are encoding containers rather than an assertion of 34 intrinsic stanzas; the Vernon witness presents the poem continuously like prose.

Rights for the medieval verbal work, Skeat's edition, Internet Archive derivatives, Bodleian photography, U-M display, DIMEV data, Carley's scholarship, and local XML serialization are recorded separately.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for aha2700
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The carrier has four DIV1 containers, a retained TITLEPAGE, and 148 PB elements. Only the BODY TYPE='text' division contains the poem.
The inherited XML AUTHOR value Joseph, of Arimathea, Saint describes the subject and is not accepted as authorship evidence.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AHA2700.0001.001
entities.description: A 2006 uncorrected OCR encoding at TEI in Libraries Level 1. It retains the 1871 title page and poem, supplies placeholders for omitted front and back matter, and links 148 TIFF page images through PB scaffolding.

date_statements.label: electronic publication
date_statements.notes: The XML publication statement and project description date the item and header creation to 2006.

notes: The 709 L elements are encoded editorial positions matching Skeat's lineation; they are not 709 separately verified physical lines in the manuscript.

The XML verbal content outside the poem is limited to the title page. Its two omitted-front-matter DIV1 placeholders, omitted-back-matter DIV1 placeholder, and 148 PB elements represent carrier structure and linked images, not extra intellectual sections of Joseph.

entities.label: Walter W. Skeat's 1871 edition of Joseph of Arimathie
alternate_labels: Joseph of Arimathie: otherwise called The Romance of the Seint Graal, or Holy Grail
entities.description: The immediate scholarly edition behind the U-M encoding. Skeat printed the poem on pp. 1–23 after carefully revising George Parker's transcript; he supplied lineation, punctuation, hyphenation, and expanded contractions while claiming the text was otherwise exact.

bibliographic.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., Joseph of Arimathie: otherwise called The Romance of the Seint Graal, or Holy Grail, Early English Text Society, Original Series 44 (London: published for the Early English Text Society by N. Trübner & Co., 1871).
bibliographic.title: Joseph of Arimathie: otherwise called The Romance of the Seint Graal, or Holy Grail
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 44
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society

notes: The containing volume has four Joseph pieces. Only the first poem on printed pp. 1–23 is the target work.
The separately bound Legends of the Holy Rood and the prose and verse appendix pieces are outside this work's source boundary.
Skeat's c. 1350 composition date is retained as history of scholarship; the work metadata follows Carley's modern c. 1375 synthesis.

entities.label: George Parker's working transcript of the Vernon Joseph
entities.description: The transcript that Bodleian assistant George Parker prepared from the Vernon witness for Skeat. Skeat calls it excellent and says the printed copy was made after his own careful revision.
notes: No public catalog record or surviving copy was identified in this research. The entity records Skeat's expressly stated workflow, not a claim that the transcript is extant.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, ff. 403ra–404vb
entities.description: The one witness recorded for DIMEV 4864 in release 2.0-alpha.1. Joseph begins defectively after missing f. 402 and occupies ff. 403ra–404vb. It ends in f. 404vb before the following Life of Pilate and Judas begins in that same folio and column.

holding.notes: The current catalog's item 363 gives Joseph at ff. 403ra–404vb; item 364 begins the following Pilate/Judas text at f. 404vb.

physical_description.extent: Modern foliation: 342 leaves plus preliminary and final leaves; incomplete, with 71 leaves thought lost from an original 413. Joseph occupies ff. 403ra–404vb in the manuscript's own current item sequence, with f. 402 missing.
physical_description.format: Large Middle English religious and didactic miscellany, normally in gatherings of eight and written in two or three columns
notes: The codex is English and associated with the West Midlands; DIMEV's manuscript data records eLALME linguistic profile 7630, Worcestershire.
The manuscript presents Joseph continuously like prose; capitals, paraphs, and punctus-like marks informed Skeat's editorial lineation.
One-witness language is scoped to DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1, not asserted as an unchanging universal census.
The work is acephalous because f. 402 is lost; Skeat nevertheless found no sign of loss at the end.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian facsimile of the Vernon Manuscript
entities.description: The complete 710-canvas Digital Bodleian object. Joseph appears on canvases 684–687, fols. 403r–404v; the fourth canvas also carries the opening of the following Pilate/Judas item after Joseph ends in column b.
entities.reproduction_format: 710 color IIIF canvases; local complete bundle of 1600-pixel-wide JPEG requests, provider manifest, and exact-source inventory
notes: Complete describes all provider canvases, including covers and flyleaves, not preservation-master resolution.
The cached end image is a whole f. 404v surface; the cataloged work portion stops within 404vb before the following item and does not claim the rest of the shared canvas.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Skeat's 1871 volume
entities.description: A complete 160-page PDF surrogate of the containing volume. The target poem occupies printed pp. 1–23, PDF pages 55–77; PDF page 78 is blank and the first appended prose Lyfe begins on PDF page 79, printed p. 25.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete 160-page PDF scan with OCR and scan-data sidecars

entities.label: Internet Archive OCR derivative for Skeat's 1871 volume
entities.description: Machine-generated plain-text OCR for the complete Internet Archive item. It is a discovery and audit aid, not the source of the U-M encoding and not an authoritative edition.

entities.label: Bodleian medieval-manuscripts catalog record for MS. Eng. poet. a. 1
entities.description: The current official codex description, including Joseph as msItem 363 and the following Pilate/Judas work as msItem 364. A commit-pinned TEI serialization is cached.

entities.label: DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 record 4864
entities.description: A versioned current census record describing 709 alliterative lines, an incomplete opening, prose manuscript presentation, Skeat pp. 1–23, and one recorded witness at Vernon ff. 403ra–404vb.
notes: This is a prerelease dataset; witness-count claims are pinned to this release.

entities.label: James P. Carley's 1996 research survey discussion of Joseph of Arimathie
entities.description: A modern scholarly synthesis at pp. 19–20: it dates the poem c. 1375, describes sole survival in Vernon and continuous-prose presentation, connects it to the Estoire del saint Graal, and reports unresolved formal and source debates.

bibliographic.citation: James P. Carley, 'England,' in Norris J. Lacy, ed., Medieval Arthurian Literature: A Guide to Recent Research (New York and London: Garland, 1996), 1–82, at 19–20.
bibliographic.title: England
bibliographic.container_title: Medieval Arthurian Literature: A Guide to Recent Research

entities.label: Anonymous Middle English Joseph of Arimathie
alternate_labels: The Romance of the Seint Graal
entities.description: The conceptual Middle English work, distinct from its single currently recorded physical witness and later editorial and digital representations. The surviving text is acephalous but apparently complete at the end.

date_statements.label: composition
date_statements.notes: Skeat's older estimate was c. 1350 and no later than 1360.

entities.label: Estoire del saint Graal
entities.description: The early Old French Vulgate Cycle narrative identified by Skeat and later scholarship as the principal source of the Middle English poem. The relation is work-to-work; no specific surviving French manuscript exemplar is asserted.

entities.label: South English Legendary: Life of Pilate and Judas
entities.description: The distinct codex item that begins in Vernon f. 404vb after Joseph ends in the same folio and column, and continues to f. 406ra. It is included only to make the shared boundary explicit.

agents.name: George Parker
notes: Skeat identifies Parker as an assistant in the Bodleian Library.

subject.description: The distinct following manuscript item beginning after Joseph in f. 404vb and continuing through f. 406ra

scope.notes: This boundary relation prevents the shared f. 404v canvas or shared column from being mistaken as wholly Joseph.

rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions where copyright term has expired
rights.basis: The anonymous work was composed in the fourteenth century and is far outside any subsisting copyright term; this does not determine rights in modern editions, encodings, descriptions, or images.
The edition was published in 1871. The Internet Archive item applies Public Domain Mark 1.0 to the scanned object; Skeat died in 1912.
notes: The Public Domain Mark is a status label, not a license.

rights.component: Internet Archive PDF, item metadata, and scan-data derivatives
rights.jurisdiction: Provider assertion and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The exact item metadata supplies Public Domain Mark 1.0 as the rights statement.

rights.jurisdiction: Provider assertion and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The OCR is a derivative file within the exact Internet Archive item to which the provider applies Public Domain Mark 1.0.

rights.component: Materials presented in the current U-M CME item
rights.basis: The current item page states that the materials are in the public domain and applies No Copyright-United States.
notes: The XML's legacy 2006 availability text says copyright or other restrictions may apply where applicable; the current item-level assertion is recorded without erasing that historical statement.
NoC-US is jurisdiction-specific and is not a worldwide license or dedication.

rights.component: Local XML serialization, markup, and repository-specific changes
rights.jurisdiction: Repository terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The pinned CME repository LICENSE contains the Unlicense text but frames its dedication as applying to software; it does not unambiguously state that the XML corpus text and markup are included. U-M's current public-domain assertion concerns materials in its item and does not by itself settle rights in later serialization or repository changes.

rights.component: Digital Bodleian manuscript photographs and cached IIIF JPEGs
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and CC BY-NC 4.0 terms
rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest attributes the photography to Bodleian Libraries and applies CC BY-NC 4.0; Digital Bodleian terms require attribution and restrict commercial use.
notes: Local caching and hardlink reuse do not grant commercial republication permission.

rights.component: Bodleian manuscript catalog prose and TEI serialization
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: No explicit item-level reuse license was found in the checked catalog TEI or catalog page during this review.
The exact Zenodo record metadata identifies license cc-by-4.0, and the release archive includes the complete Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license text.

rights.component: Carley's chapter text and publisher preview PDF
rights.jurisdiction: Applicable copyright law and publisher terms
rights.basis: The 1996 scholarly chapter and current publisher preview remain under copyright; public preview access is not an open license.

editorial_practices.description: Skeat says he expanded contractions while otherwise attempting an exact text.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Continuous-prose witness with capitals, paraphs, and points that suggest verse divisions
editorial_practices.representation: Editor supplied lineation, punctuation, and hyphenation
editorial_practices.description: Skeat used manuscript cues to divide the continuously written witness into alliterative long lines and supplied punctuation and hyphenation.
notes: The modern verse form and manuscript prose layout must both remain discoverable.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A four-piece 1871 volume with preface, notes, glossaries, and indexes
editorial_practices.representation: Verbal encoding of the title page and first poem only; omitted-matter placeholders and 148 PB links retain carrier scaffolding
editorial_practices.description: U-M encoded the title page and Joseph poem while representing other pages as omitted front/back matter and linked page breaks.
notes: The omitted carrier matter is not part of the intellectual work Joseph of Arimathie.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/aha2700.xml at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: complete file; HEADER, FRONT, TITLEPAGE, BODY, and BACK
evidence.summary: Exact evidence for repository identity and hash, U-M identifier and 2006 OCR/TEI statement, Skeat bibliographic source, title-page selection, four DIV1 containers, 148 PB elements, 709 BODY L positions, 34 LG containers, eight manuscript milestones, and omitted-front/back structure.
notes: A target-scoped audit confirmed 709 nonblank BODY L elements; all 172 explicit N values match their positional lines through 709.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME LICENSE at pinned commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Contains the Unlicense text but expressly frames the dedication in terms of software, leaving its application to corpus XML text and markup unclear.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, AHA2700.0001.001.
evidence.locator: item header, full-text display, and rights statement
evidence.summary: Current official display for the electronic poem and identifier; it states that the item materials are in the public domain and applies No Copyright-United States.
notes: The inherited AUTHOR label naming Joseph of Arimathea is not accepted as evidence that the legendary subject authored the anonymous poem.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., Joseph of Arimathie, Early English Text Society, Original Series 44 (London: N. Trübner, 1871).
evidence.locator: preface pp. vii–xvi, especially vii–ix; poem pp. 1–23 / PDF pages 55–77
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the four-piece volume boundary, missing f. 402, Parker's transcript and Skeat's careful revision, supplied lineation/punctuation/hyphenation and expanded contractions, 709 extant lines, intact endpoint, historical dating and dialect argument, and dependence on the Estoire del saint Graal.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive item JosephOfArimathie, complete PDF, metadata, OCR, and scan-data derivatives.
evidence.locator: complete item file inventory; PDF pages 55–79; Public Domain Mark metadata
evidence.summary: Confirms the 160-page complete scan, 1871 publication, poem boundary at printed pp. 1–23, blank separator, appended-work start, available PDF/OCR/scan-data derivatives, and Public Domain Mark 1.0.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, item 363; TEI source pinned at medieval-mss commit cd36dce9a104617f960dab2c97ea78f95f917496.
evidence.locator: msItem 363, ff. 403ra–404vb; msItem 364 begins f. 404vb; codex origin, date, and physical description
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for the defective Joseph item, exact locus and explicit, adjacent Pilate/Judas boundary, c. 1390–1400 codex date with 1381 lower bound, English origin, physical extent, and missing f. 402.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF object and structure ranges for the Vernon Manuscript.
evidence.locator: 710 canvases total; Joseph canvases 684–687, images/000684.jpg–images/000687.jpg, ff. 403r–404v; ranges LOG_0371 and LOG_0372
evidence.summary: ZIP, inventory, manifest, and image inspection confirm complete 710-canvas coverage, the four-surface Joseph range, mid-sentence opening, continuous-prose presentation, and the shared f. 404v boundary where Joseph ends before Pilate/Judas begins in the same column.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, Terms of Use, together with the exact Vernon IIIF manifest attribution and license.
evidence.locator: terms of use, permitted use, prohibited use, and item manifest license
evidence.summary: The item manifest attributes the photographs to Bodleian Libraries and applies CC BY-NC 4.0; the provider terms require attribution and restrict commercial reuse.

evidence.citation: Linne R. Mooney, Daniel W. Mosser, Ian Cornelius, and Michael Johnston, The Digital Index of Middle English Verse: Data Files and Documentation, version 2.0-alpha.1 (2026), record 4864.
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml record-4864 and wit-4864-1; data/Manuscripts.xml BodEngPoeta1; LICENSE
evidence.summary: Versioned evidence for DIMEV 4864 / IMEV-NIMEV 3117.4, 709 alliterative lines, saint's-life subjects, incomplete opening, prose manuscript presentation, Skeat pp. 1–23, one recorded Vernon witness at ff. 403ra–404vb, Worcestershire linguistic profile 7630, and CC BY 4.0 release terms; the exact Zenodo API record identifies license cc-by-4.0 and the archive includes the full license text.
notes: Version 2.0-alpha.1 is a prerelease; the witness census is scoped to this exact release.

evidence.citation: James P. Carley, 'England,' in Norris J. Lacy, ed., Medieval Arthurian Literature: A Guide to Recent Research (New York and London: Garland, 1996), 1–82, at 19–20.
evidence.summary: Modern synthesis for c. 1375 dating, survival only in Vernon, continuous-prose presentation, Skeat's alliterative lineation, derivation from the Estoire, Lawton's debated draft-form theory, weak support for a Queste contribution, and hagiographic and didactic readings.

open_questions.question: Does George Parker's working transcript survive, and if so where is it held?
next_steps: Search Bodleian administrative and manuscript-department archives for Parker's papers or transcripts.
Search Walter W. Skeat archival holdings and correspondence catalogs.
Retain the intermediary as survival unknown unless an archival record establishes its status.
notes: Skeat's preface establishes that the transcript existed before publication but does not state its later disposition.

open_questions.question: How much text and which narrative material preceded the defective opening after loss of f. 402?
next_steps: Consult David Lawton's 1983 edition and later codicological studies.
Do not convert Skeat's fewer-than-one-hundred-line estimate into a normalized extent without stronger evidence.

open_questions.question: How should Lawton's first-draft metrical theory and proposed Queste source contribution be evaluated against the later criticism Carley summarizes?
next_steps: Review the specific contemporary reviews cited by Carley before asserting a more granular metrical model or a Queste lineage edge.

open_questions.question: Does the CME repository's Unlicense expressly cover corpus XML text and markup, rather than software alone?
next_steps: Seek an explicit scope statement from the CME repository maintainer.
Continue treating the local serialization rights as unknown while preserving U-M's current NoC-US item assertion separately.
